a: A container is your app packaged together with everything it needs to run, like its code and libraries. It runs the same way on any machine, so 'works on my laptop' also works on the server.
fs: A container is an isolated, portable bundle of an app and its dependencies that runs identically across environments.
fj: Confusing a container with a virtual machine. A container shares the host's OS kernel and is far lighter than a full VM.

a: A Pod is the smallest thing Kubernetes runs, and it wraps one container (sometimes a few that belong together). Kubernetes never runs a bare container directly; it always runs it inside a Pod.
fs: A Pod is the atomic deploy unit: one or more tightly-coupled containers that share a network address and lifecycle.
fj: Thinking a Pod equals one app forever. Pods are disposable; they get killed and recreated, often with a new IP each time.

a: A Node is one machine (a server or VM) in your Kubernetes cluster that actually runs Pods. A cluster is just a group of Nodes working together under one control plane.
fs: A Node is a worker machine that provides CPU and memory; the scheduler places Pods onto Nodes that have room.
fj: Mixing up a Node and a Pod. A Node is the physical/virtual machine; a Pod is the app running on top of that machine.

a: A Deployment is where you declare how many copies of your app you want and which container image to run. Kubernetes then creates the Pods, keeps that count alive, and rolls out updates gradually when you change the image.
fs: A Deployment manages a desired set of identical Pods: it handles scaling, self-healing, and safe rolling updates.
fj: Creating Pods by hand instead. A loose Pod won't come back if it dies; a Deployment recreates Pods to hold the target count.

a: A Service gives a stable name and address to a group of Pods, so other apps can reach them even as Pods come and go. It also spreads incoming traffic across the healthy Pods behind it.
fs: A Service is a stable network endpoint that load-balances to a changing set of Pods selected by labels.
fj: Calling a Pod's own IP directly. Pod IPs change when Pods restart; you talk to the Service, which always finds the live Pods.
